claim: A 2012 review documents the reduction in Bangladesh cyclone mortality from 300,000-500,000 in 1970 to 3,406 in 2007 Cyclone Sidr through the establishment of the Cyclone Preparedness Programme, early warning dissemination, and cyclone shelter construction.

source.title: Reduced death rates from cyclones in Bangladesh: what more needs to be done?

method: Reviewed WHO Bulletin article mortality analysis, CPP description, and shelter infrastructure assessment.
limitations: Historical mortality data before 1970 is estimated, not precise.
Review does not quantify modern false-alarm rates.

claim: The IFRC documents Bangladesh's Cyclone Preparedness Programme as a globally recognized community-based early warning model involving Red Crescent volunteers in coastal districts for cyclone alert dissemination, evacuation support, and shelter management.

source.title: Bangladesh disaster risk and response — ReliefWeb / UN OCHA

method: Reviewed IFRC Bangladesh country page for CPP program description, Red Crescent volunteer role, and early warning dissemination model.
limitations: IFRC page provides program description, not specific operational statistics (volunteer counts, shelter numbers) which require official CPP government documentation at cpp.gov.bd — a source that was not programmatically accessible during verify:sources on 2026-06-07.
IFRC framing reflects partnership perspective; operational gaps and coverage limitations require direct CPP and government data to verify.

claim: A 2023 verification study of 24 North Indian Ocean tropical cyclones (2016-2020) finds direct position errors of 68.12 km at 12h, 91.79 km at 24h, 149.8 km at 48h, and 232.36 km at 72h over the NIO as a whole, with landfall position errors lower over the Bay of Bengal than the Arabian Sea, and intensity errors magnifying from 4 to 12 m/s with lead time.

source.title: Verification of tropical cyclone motion and rainfall forecast over North Indian Ocean

method: Reviewed abstract and results from this Journal of Earth System Science article. The study simulates 24 TCs from 2016-2020 over the NIO using quasi-operational WRF and reports DPE at 12/24/48/72h lead times, landfall position errors by basin, and intensity errors. These figures are more recent and more specific than the previously cited Mohapatra et al. 2020 study (DOI 10.1007/s00703-020-00745-8), which has been retracted or removed and returns HTTP 404 from both Springer and doi.org as of 2026-06-28.
limitations: DPE values are NIO-wide averages and may differ for Bay of Bengal specifically, though the study notes landfall errors are lower over the BoB than the Arabian Sea.
The 24 TCs span 2016-2020; model improvements since 2020 may have reduced errors.
The study uses quasi-operational WRF model forecasts, not official IMD operational forecasts, so error statistics may differ from IMD operational performance.
Intensity errors of 4-12 m/s are model-dependent and may vary across operational centers using different intensity estimation techniques.

claim: NOAA's International Best Track Archive for Climate Stewardship (IBTrACS) provides the most complete public global tropical cyclone best-track dataset, combining all WMO-sanctioned RSMC and TCWC best tracks plus JTWC and other agency records at 6-hourly intervals from 1842 to present, suitable as the historical cyclone track verification baseline for Bay of Bengal cyclone analysis.

source.title: NOAA National Centers for Environmental Information — IBTrACS

method: Reviewed the NOAA NCEI IBTrACS product page for dataset description, temporal coverage (1842-present), spatial coverage (global), temporal resolution (6-hourly with some 3-hourly), contributing agencies (WMO RSMCs, TCWCs, JTWC), data format (CSV, NetCDF, shapefile), version history, and known limitations. Confirmed Bay of Bengal coverage includes IMD and BMD best tracks.
limitations: Best-track data merges records from multiple agencies with different intensity estimation methods; intensity values before the satellite era (pre-1965) are less reliable.
6-hourly temporal resolution may miss rapid intensification events between synoptic times.
IBTrACS version updates can revise historical tracks; the specific version used must be cited in any downstream verification.
Landfall position and timing are interpolated from 6-hourly points and may differ from actual landfall by tens of kilometers.

claim: The US Joint Typhoon Warning Center best-track archive provides tropical cyclone track and intensity records for the Western North Pacific and Bay of Bengal basins at 6-hourly intervals from 1945 to present, using the Dvorak technique and satellite-based intensity estimates, and serves as an independent cross-reference for IBTrACS and IMD best tracks in the Bay of Bengal.

source.title: Joint Typhoon Warning Center — Tropical Cyclone Best Track Data

method: Reviewed the JTWC website for best-track data availability, data format, temporal coverage, basin coverage, and documentation. JTWC provides best-track files for the Western North Pacific (which includes the Bay of Bengal) in text format with 6-hourly position, intensity (1-minute sustained winds), and central pressure.
limitations: JTWC uses 1-minute sustained wind averaging, while IMD and BMD use 3-minute and 10-minute averaging respectively; direct intensity comparisons require unit conversion and are not one-to-one.

JTWC best tracks are not the official WMO-sanctioned record for the Bay of Bengal; IMD (RSMC New Delhi) is the official source, so JTWC should be used as cross-reference, not as the primary track for operational verification.
Pre-satellite era tracks (1945-1965) have larger position and intensity uncertainty.
The JTWC website has intermittent availability and may block automated access; data should be mirrored locally for reproducibility.

claim: WorldPop provides gridded population estimates for Bangladesh at approximately 100m resolution using census-derived top-down disaggregation, suitable for calculating coastal population exposure within and outside cyclone warning zones, but the most recent Bangladesh census was 2022 and WorldPop estimates may lag official updates.
method: Reviewed the WorldPop data catalog for Bangladesh-specific population products, spatial resolution (100m), temporal coverage, methodology (top-down disaggregation using census and covariates), and data availability. Confirmed Bangladesh country-level and gridded products are available.
limitations: Bangladesh conducted its most recent census in 2022; WorldPop estimates based on earlier censuses may undercount populations in rapidly urbanizing coastal areas.

Top-down disaggregation models assume population distribution patterns from covariates (nighttime lights, land cover, roads) that may not capture informal settlements or char land populations accurately.
100m resolution is sufficient for union-level exposure analysis but may miss fine-scale population clustering on islands and coastal chars.
Cyclone shelter capacity and road network data are not included in WorldPop; exposure analysis requires integration with shelter and infrastructure datasets.

claim: The GEBCO global ocean bathymetry dataset provides seafloor depth at 15 arc-second (approximately 450m) resolution globally, including the Bay of Bengal continental shelf and coastal Bangladesh, and serves as a primary input for storm surge models that require high-resolution bathymetry in the shallow, funnel-shaped northern Bay of Bengal where surge amplification occurs.

source.title: GEBCO — General Bathymetric Chart of the Oceans

method: Reviewed the GEBCO website for gridded bathymetry product specifications, spatial resolution (15 arc-second global grid), data sources (multibeam surveys, satellite altimetry, single-beam soundings), version history (GEBCO 2024), data format (NetCDF), and licensing (free to use). Assessed coverage of the Bay of Bengal continental shelf and coastal Bangladesh region.
limitations: 15 arc-second resolution is coarser than the fine-scale coastal geometry that controls surge amplification in funnel-shaped estuaries; storm surge models for district-level verification may require higher-resolution local bathymetry from BMD or Bangladesh Inland Water Transport Authority.
GEBCO bathymetry in shallow coastal areas relies on satellite altimetry, which degrades in water depths under 20m; the near-shore zone where surge is highest may have significant depth errors.
GEBCO does not include topographic elevation data for overland surge inundation; storm surge models need a separate digital elevation model (e.g., SRTM, MERIT DEM) for overland flow.
The dataset is updated annually; the specific GEBCO version used must be cited in any downstream surge model verification.
